text:
It is a well-known conspiracy orchestrated by gravity, which has had a grudge against butter ever since butter refused to dissolve in water and made gravity look bad in front of all the other forces. Gravity cannot directly attack butter while it sits safely on the counter, so it waits patiently for the one vulnerable moment when toast is nudged off a plate and begins to fall.

The toast, you see, always starts with the buttered side facing up, because humans are optimistic creatures who like to see the good side of things. As the toast begins its fateful tumble off the edge of the table, it rotates exactly half a turn before hitting the floor, placing the buttered side down. This is not an accident. Table height on planet Earth, through millions of years of evolution and furniture design, has converged on precisely the altitude required to give toast enough time to rotate one hundred and eighty degrees and no more. If tables were taller, toast would complete a full rotation and land butter-side up. If tables were shorter, toast would land on its edge, which would be miraculous but inconvenient. The universe simply refuses to allow either outcome.

There is also a secondary mechanism involving the observation that the more expensive the butter, the faster gravity acts. Scientists have not confirmed this, but scientists have not disproved it either, and everyone who has ever dropped an entire stick of Irish butter on a white rug knows it to be true in their heart.

The final contributing factor is that the universe is fundamentally a dramatic place that prefers a good story to a convenient one, and a piece of toast landing safely with its buttered side up has no story worth telling. A piece of toast landing butter-side down on a freshly mopped floor, however, is a tale that gets retold at breakfast tables for generations. The universe, it turns out, is optimizing for content.
